identity.credential_name: Master of Information Management and Systems (MIMS)

positioning.positioning_summary: UC Berkeley’s MIMS is positioned as a two-year, full-time professional master’s for aspiring technology leaders and information professionals, with a strongly interdisciplinary curriculum spanning computer science, data systems/AI, human-centered design/UX, policy, ethics, law, and management. The program also signals a practical, career-oriented focus through hands-on project work, internships, and outcomes in UX, product, data, and tech leadership, while explicitly framing its work around ethical technology and social impact.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIMS is a two-year, full-time, 48-semester-unit professional degree. The first year is mostly a required core, followed by electives and additional breadth requirements in the second year, with a required final project in the last semester. The program is flexible overall and supports specialization through electives and thematic focus areas, but it does not present formal tracks or concentrations.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course introducing information organization and retrieval. Covers conceptual modeling, semantic representation, vocabulary and metadata design, classification, standardization, and information retrieval practices and applications.
learning_outcomes: Understand foundations of information organization and retrieval
Apply conceptual modeling and semantic representation
Design vocabularies, metadata, and classification schemes
Use information retrieval practices and related technologies
Analyze textual and non-textual information computationally

core_courses.course_summary: Required 3-unit core course introducing social issues around information and information technology, including technology’s impact on society and social groups.
learning_outcomes: Explain how information technologies affect social groups and social interaction.
Analyze how society shapes the technologies that are produced.
Discuss theories of technology in society.
Identify workplace, automation, and networked sociability issues.
Use social scientific methods to examine information technology problems.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course examining legal and ethical issues in information and communication technologies, including privacy, freedom of expression, consumer protection, cybersecurity, and copyright.
learning_outcomes: Analyze legal and ethical issues in information technologies.
Explain how policy frameworks shape technology design and use.
Evaluate privacy, copyright, and consumer protection concerns.
Reflect on the ethical impact of information professionals' work.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course in programming and computation. The 2-unit course introduces Python programming for computer science, data science, and information management, with emphasis on problem decomposition, program efficiency, and good programming style.
learning_outcomes: Write interactive Python programs
Analyze data and process text
Draw graphics and manipulate images
Simulate physical systems
Apply problem decomposition and efficient coding practices

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to Data Structures and Analytics
core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing data structures and data analysis in Python; 2 units; forms the second half of the INFO 206A/206B sequence.
learning_outcomes: Represent structured data sets
Manipulate and analyze data in Python
Apply best practices for writing code
Build on INFO 206A prerequisites

core_courses.course_title: Directed Group Work on Final Project
core_courses.course_summary: Required culminating final project completed in the fourth semester. The project integrates skills and concepts from the MIMS program and may take the form of a research paper or professionally oriented applied work.
learning_outcomes: Integrate skills and concepts learned in the MIMS program.
Formulate and carry out a sustained, coherent project.
Produce a tangible work product.
Manage a significant project from start to finish.
Present work in written and oral form.

electives.summary: Electives are broad and flexible, with students drawing from the I School catalog and other UC Berkeley departments to build specialization in areas such as UX, data science, policy, entrepreneurship, and privacy/security. The program requires at least 40 units from the Info course catalog, may include up to 6 units of individual or group study, and allows up to 8 units from other departments to count toward the degree.

positioning.positioning_summary: The Berkeley MDes is positioned as a three-semester, interdisciplinary professional graduate program in design for emerging technologies that connects technical rigor, design theory, and social practice, with strong emphasis on career preparation, real-world application, and socially responsible design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Berkeley MDes is a studio-based, three-semester professional master’s program completed over about 16 months and requiring a minimum of 39 units. The curriculum combines eight named required core courses, a summer Design@Large professional development experience, and four electives drawn from technical, entrepreneurship, social practice, and open UC Berkeley courses. The materials show a clear year-by-year sample sequence, but the program also allows some flexibility in elective timing and selection.

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational MDes core course that interweaves practical design methods with readings and lectures on the history of design and technology.
learning_outcomes: Use design methods to frame and solve problems
Apply noticing, observing, framing, and reframing
Connect design practice with history of design and technology
Develop critique and reflection skills across design contexts

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational course in design and technology frameworks. Students use human-centered design, research, concept generation, prototyping, critique, and a semester-long hardware-plus-digital project to create a product-service system.
learning_outcomes: Apply human-centered design methods to emerging technologies.
Generate and refine concepts through research and prototyping.
Build fluency with fabrication and microcontroller-based technologies.
Use critique and iteration to improve design solutions.
Create a product-service system addressing a well-defined need.

core_courses.course_summary: Colloquium-style course exploring a yearly design topic through invited lectures, readings, discussion, and written reflections.
learning_outcomes: Articulate new methods for connecting conceptual knowledge with technical skills.
Integrate ideas from multiple perspectives and worldviews.
Develop thoughtful questions and discussion points from advance readings.
Write reflective responses to lectures and discussions.
Engage contemporary design challenges at different scales of practice.

core_courses.course_summary: Introductory design studio that orients students to studio culture, iterative making, critique, communication, and portfolio development.
learning_outcomes: Understand the culture and life of a working studio
Develop ideas through feedback, critique, and refinement
Work with a variety of materials and media
Practice visual and verbal communication
Begin building a portfolio of design work

core_courses.course_summary: A substantial studio course focused on designing emerging technologies through iterative project work, critique, and technical production.
learning_outcomes: Design products, services, and experiences for emerging technologies
Iterate concepts through critique and refinement
Apply technical production skills in a studio context
Develop solutions to well-defined design problems
Integrate hardware and digital technologies in project work

core_courses.course_summary: Hands-on thesis studio focused on fabrication, building technologies, and fundamental design production skills in a studio environment.
learning_outcomes: Develop a thesis project in a studio setting
Apply fabrication and building technologies
Produce a real-world application or solution
Work with external partners or faculty-led themes
Advance fundamental design production skills

core_courses.course_summary: A 1-unit online seminar supporting the MDes thesis process and broader Design@Large reflection.
learning_outcomes: Develop an important thesis question for design.
Produce a formal written thesis document.
Connect socio-cultural investigation with technical exploration.
Reflect on and document the final studio project.
Situate thesis work within broader design practice.

core_courses.course_summary: Culminating MDes portfolio course in which students compile and reflect on at least four meaningful pieces of work, including studio, prior coursework, and Design@Large experience.
learning_outcomes: Compile a coherent portfolio of MDes work
Select projects that demonstrate key learning objectives
Present deeper investigations from prior classes
Reflect on studio and Design@Large experiences

electives.summary: The MDes requires four electives: one technical elective, one entrepreneurship or social practice elective, and two open electives. The approved lists are broad and cross-disciplinary, spanning computing, engineering, planning, new media, and design-related topics. The open electives may be any 3- or 4-unit upper-division or graduate UC Berkeley course, subject to availability. The structure supports individualized emphasis, but the program does not advertise formal tracks or concentrations.

identity.credential_name: Master of Fine Arts in Media Design Practices

positioning.positioning_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is positioned as an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated design program centered on emerging technologies, critical frameworks, and experimental studio practice. It emphasizes research, prototyping, and fieldwork, with explicit social-impact and global-context framing, and prepares students for professional roles at the intersection of technology, culture, and design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated graduate program organized around a sequenced studio curriculum leading to thesis work. The evidence indicates two- and three-year pathways, with the three-year option adding preparatory undergraduate-level terms before the standard MGx track. The program emphasizes critical thinking, creative technology, prototyping, research, fieldwork, and thesis studio work; no distinct concentration tracks are clearly described in the fetched material.

core_courses.course_summary: First-term development-year course in the three-year MDP track focused on critical framing and worldview analysis; 3 units.
learning_outcomes: Analyze critical worldviews shaping media design practice.
Frame design problems through critical and cultural perspectives.
Develop a reflective stance toward technology and society.
Connect design decisions to broader social and ethical contexts.

core_courses.course_summary: Fall-term thesis-writing and dissemination course in the Media Design Practices MFA. Students develop the written component of the thesis, situate their work in discourse, and prepare materials for sharing design practice through venues such as social media, journals, and biennales.
learning_outcomes: Develop the written component of a thesis
Use writing as a generative design tool
Situate work within critical discourse
Translate practice for public dissemination
Prepare thesis-related promotional materials

core_courses.course_summary: A required creative technology studio term in the MDP sequence, focused on emerging technologies, prototyping, and experimental making.
learning_outcomes: Investigate emerging technologies in design practice.
Develop prototypes through experimental making.
Apply creative technology to design challenges.
Use research and fieldwork to inform design decisions.
Create new modes of storytelling and installation.

core_courses.course_summary: Thesis-year studio in the three-year Media Design Practices track, where students develop thesis work after the preparatory terms.
learning_outcomes: Develop thesis work through studio practice
Apply research and fieldwork to design problems
Use emerging technologies in experimental making
Create new modes of storytelling and installation
Envision future design responses to social issues

electives.summary: The program overview does not describe a formal elective structure or named elective categories for Media Design Practices. The evidence only indicates a sequenced studio curriculum with a thesis year and additional preparatory terms in the three-year option, but it does not clearly state how electives are selected or whether they support specialization.

llm_rationales.note: The program page describes the CD program as 'fundamentally interdisciplinary,' at 'the nexus of design and computation,' and says students develop a 'unique research agenda' and produce a thesis with a 'novel contribution to knowledge.'

Supported tags: interdisciplinary (explicitly 'fundamentally interdisciplinary'); research (research agenda, thesis, novel contribution to knowledge); hci_adjacent (draws from computer science, robotics, machine learning, HCI); emerging_technologies (machine learning/AI, virtual and augmented reality, robotics); physical_making (robotically-supported construction, fabrication, prototyping); critical_speculative_design (critical inquiry, challenge disciplinary boundaries); social_impact (reimagines material, sociotechnical, and environmental relations).

The CodeLab page reinforces interdisciplinary and research positioning, describing the lab as 'fundamentally curious, interdisciplinary, and experimental' and highlighting research areas including architectural robotics, artificial intelligence, digital heritage, generative fabrication, and computational structural design.

Omitted professional because the page emphasizes research-focused preparation and scholarly contribution more strongly than direct industry readiness; omitted fine_arts_adjacent because the evidence mentions art and creative disciplines, but not a primary fine-arts positioning for the program itself.

identity.credential_name: Master of Science in Computational Design

positioning.positioning_summary: Carnegie Mellon’s Computational Design program is positioned as a highly interdisciplinary, research-focused graduate program at the nexus of design and computation. It emphasizes critical technical practice, novel research contributions, and work that spans architecture, engineering, art, humanities, and computing, while also foregrounding emerging technologies, digital methods, physical fabrication, and socially and environmentally aware design inquiry.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Computational Design program is a two-year, research-focused MS/PhD pathway centered on interdisciplinary study at the intersection of design and computation. The evidence describes a lean sequence of research seminars, selective courses, special topics, independent studies, and a thesis, with students building individualized plans of study under faculty guidance. The program emphasizes technical, conceptual, historical, and critical inquiry, and the MS culminates in a substantial thesis.

electives.summary: Electives are highly flexible but guided by faculty and the program’s research orientation. The MS curriculum uses research seminars, selective courses, special topics seminars, independent studies, and a broad pool of eligible extra-departmental courses to let students build a personalized path; the evidence does not describe fixed elective buckets or named elective lists for this program page.

identity.program_name: Master of Design in Design for Interactions

positioning.positioning_summary: The MDes in Design for Interactions is positioned as a rigorous, research-informed design program for students with prior design backgrounds and professional experience, centered on interaction design, systems thinking, design research, and thesis work. It emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration, professional practice, and social impact through services, social innovation, sustainability, and future-oriented design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master of Design in Design for Interactions is a two-year, four-semester program built around required studios, seminars, labs, thesis preparation, and electives. The curriculum is described semester by semester, with first-year study focused on interaction, services, and social innovation, and second-year study centered on thesis research, development, and public defense. The page also indicates that students may take electives in the School of Design or across Carnegie Mellon, including outside design, to complement their skills and knowledge.

core_courses.course_summary: First-year core study in the MDes program introduces interaction design practice, design research, systems thinking, and methods for designing services and social innovation concepts.
learning_outcomes: Understand interaction design practice in context
Apply design research methods to complex systems
Prototype preferred futures for products and services
Work with communities, organizations, and stakeholders
Develop a personal design voice through studio work

core_courses.course_summary: Project-based course on service design fundamentals, including analyzing and improving existing services and developing new service experiences. Students work across digital interactions and physical experiences, hypothesize, experiment, build, test, pivot, and pitch a service with value to a particular audience.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamentals of service design
Analyze and improve existing services
Integrate digital interactions with physical experiences
Use hypothesis-driven experimentation and testing
Pitch a service for a specific audience

core_courses.course_id: Year 2 core — thesis defense and completion
core_courses.course_summary: The final semester brings the thesis project to fruition through synthesis, documentation, public presentation, and defense.

electives.summary: Electives are flexible rather than locked to a single list: students take a design elective or a course outside design in the first year, and again in the second year, to complement their skills and knowledge. The page recommends electives in policy, business, service or social innovation, interaction or communication design, and professional writing, and also notes that students may take courses across Carnegie Mellon, including in other schools and departments.

Design in Action appears in both the full-time and part-time tables, so it is listed once in the curriculum core and also reflected in the sequence.

positioning.positioning_summary: The program is positioned as a computer science master’s concentration that bridges CS with digital arts and visual computing. It emphasizes a rigorous CS foundation, foundational digital arts coursework, and research/thesis work in areas such as computer graphics, HCI, digital fabrication, digital art and media, computer vision, VR, and AR. The framing is strongly interdisciplinary, STEM-adjacent, research-oriented, and connected to digital/fine-arts practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MS in Computer Science with a concentration in Digital Arts is described as a two-year program that combines technical computer science study, digital arts coursework, and research/thesis work. Evidence indicates a thesis-based graduate path with a required thesis defense, but the fetched material does not provide a full term-by-term plan or a complete named course list for the concentration.

positioning.positioning_summary: TU Delft’s MSc Integrated Product Design is positioned as an advanced, research-informed design program that blends design, technology, and engineering to create user-centered, innovative, sustainable, and ethical products and product-service combinations. The program also explicitly highlights emerging technologies such as AI, smart textiles, bio-design, digital fabrication, and computational design, while emphasizing balance among user, business, and societal interests.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MSc Integrated Product Design is a two-year, 120 ECTS programme with a structured first-year core, a theme-based second-year component, a 30 ECTS free elective semester, and a 30 ECTS graduation project. The curriculum is organized around faculty core courses, programme core courses, design studios, three theme courses chosen from an offer, and flexible electives that can include faculty courses, other TU Delft or Dutch university courses, internships, or study abroad.

positioning.positioning_summary: Harvard GSD’s Master in Design Studies is positioned as a research-based, interdisciplinary design program focused on urgent contemporary issues and social impact. The program emphasizes design research and scholarship, flexible domain-based study, and work that connects design to public service, academia, NGOs, and advanced practice; the Publics domain further highlights socio-spatial design, planning, advocacy, and the common good.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master in Design Studies is a four-semester, 72-unit program organized by domain (Ecologies, Mediums, Narratives, or Publics). Students complete a required first-semester proseminar in their chosen domain, a 12-unit set of approved distributional electives in the first year, a required 8-unit collaborative open project in the third semester, and a largely elective fourth semester. The curriculum is flexible within each domain but still has a clear semester-by-semester structure.

core_courses.course_title: Proseminar in Ecologies: Regenerative, Interrelated, Evolving
core_courses.course_summary: Required first-semester proseminar for the Ecologies domain. The course introduces students to research being pursued by GSD faculty, across Harvard schools, the Loeb Fellows, and practitioners from many disciplines.
learning_outcomes: Understand the Ecologies domain’s core concerns and research agenda.
Engage research pursued by GSD faculty and affiliated scholars.
Situate design questions across ecological, technical, and social systems.
Develop a foundation for an individualized domain trajectory.

core_courses.course_title: Proseminar in Mediums: On Making Culture, Technology, and Art
core_courses.course_summary: First-semester required proseminar for the Mediums domain. Explores the landscape of design technologies and technologically driven design, with critical attention to making culture, technology, and art.
learning_outcomes: Critically examine design technologies and technologically driven design.
Analyze how mediums shape ideas, experiences, and environments.
Understand the role of culture, technology, and art in design.
Develop a critical perspective on emerging tools and methods.

core_courses.course_title: Proseminar in Narratives: Word and Image as Narrative Structure
core_courses.course_summary: First-semester required proseminar in the Narratives domain; examines word-image relations through aesthetic philosophy, literary criticism, media studies, and art and architectural history.
learning_outcomes: Analyze word-image relations across design and media contexts.
Compare critical perspectives from philosophy, criticism, and history.
Evaluate narrative structure through visual and textual forms.
Connect design-related themes to broader cultural debates.

core_courses.course_title: Proseminar in Publics: Of the Public. In the Public. By the Public.

electives.summary: In the first year, students must take a minimum of 12 units of approved distributional electives from the list for their chosen domain, and those electives may be completed in the second year with Domain Head approval. The program is interdisciplinary and encourages cross-registration across the GSD, other Harvard schools, and MIT, while the fourth semester in the domain plans is largely or entirely general electives.

positioning.positioning_summary: IIT Institute of Design’s M.Des. is positioned as a STEM-designated, professionally oriented graduate design program for strategic designers who work across complex business, civic, and social challenges. The program emphasizes human-centered and systems design, collaborative real-world projects, and impact in private, public, and nonprofit sectors, while also welcoming applicants without formal design training.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The M.Des. is a two-year, 54-credit program with a structured core and substantial elective flexibility. Required study includes a small set of core courses, one 4-credit choice from a specified course set, and a large elective block drawn mainly from 500- and 600-level IDN/IDX courses. Students without prior design preparation may need Foundation courses, and some international students may take English + Design courses concurrently in the first two semesters. The curriculum is organized around user research, communication, systems thinking, strategy, and workshops, with adviser-guided course selection and optional outside-of-ID electives allowed in limited amounts.

learning_outcomes: Use primary and secondary research methods
Apply quantitative and qualitative approaches

positioning.positioning_summary: Harvard’s Master in Design Engineering is positioned as a collaborative, interdisciplinary program that bridges design and engineering to tackle complex real-world and societal challenges. The program emphasizes systems-level problem solving, innovation, and leadership, with explicit ties to sustainability, urbanization, resource scarcity, and other large-scale public-interest issues.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program is a four-semester, highly structured curriculum organized by domain. Students choose one of four domains and complete a required first-semester proseminar, DES-3105 Futures By Design in the second semester, an 8-unit Open Project in the third semester, and general electives concentrated across the third and fourth semesters. The evidence also shows a minimum 12-unit distributional elective requirement drawn from approved domain lists, with cross-registration allowed but constrained.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: ITP is a 60-credit, typically four-semester graduate program organized into three tiers: foundation courses, a large elective block, and a final thesis project. The curriculum is strongly structured at the start with mandatory foundation courses, then becomes more flexible through seminars, workshops, electives inside and outside ITP, and a 4-credit thesis.

positioning.positioning_summary: The M.S. in Emerging Technologies is positioned as a flexible, AI-centered, cross-disciplinary degree for students to build a personalized curriculum across multiple technology areas. The program explicitly emphasizes AI fluency, interdisciplinary electives spanning Tandon departments, and career-oriented preparation for an evolving tech-driven job market.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: NYU Tandon’s Emerging Technologies M.S. is a 30-credit, highly flexible program built around a chosen concentration, a capstone, and electives. The evidence shows multiple concentration options and sample study plans, with some concentration-specific core/capstone requirements and broad elective choice across departments; students may also switch concentration once under stated conditions.

electives.summary: Electives are broad and largely self-directed. In the Emerging Technologies M.S., students take 18 credits of electives from a long approved list, with advisor approval possible for courses outside the list, and courses used for core or capstone cannot double-count. In the broader program description, electives are also organized around concentration areas, letting students mix courses across departments and use electives to support their chosen specialization.

positioning.positioning_summary: NYU Integrated Design and Media is positioned as an interdisciplinary, research-active program that blends creative practice with engineering and emerging media technologies. It emphasizes cross-disciplinary work, social and ethical responsibility, and hands-on project-based production, with clear ties to digital media, interaction, and professional practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The evidence shows a project-based, interdisciplinary curriculum with a clear foundation-to-advanced progression in several programs, but not a single fully specified degree plan. NYU Game Design and Integrated Media/IDM materials emphasize foundational literacy, design, programming, visual/audio production, and advanced studios; IMB shows a foundation-plus-elective-plus-capstone structure; Dance and Technology shows a small required sequence and, for graduate students, a concentration path; the Tandon IDM pages show a structured BS/MS curriculum with required core areas and a thesis sequence.

electives.summary: The evidence shows a mix of open and structured electives. Game Design includes many topical studios and advanced project courses that function as elective pathways, while IMB explicitly labels many courses as IMB/IMA electives and allows business electives in related areas. IDM’s BS includes upper-level electives across user experience, augmented reality, motion capture, and related areas, and the minor requires a minimum number of DM electives. Dance and Technology also allows graduate students to take the curriculum as individual courses or as a concentration, with additional cross-departmental options.

positioning.positioning_summary: The mpd² program is positioned as a professional, industry-facing master’s for experienced practitioners who want to lead product design and development. Its messaging emphasizes cross-disciplinary training in business, design, technology, and product development, with a strong focus on emerging technology and AI systems, and on preparing graduates for senior leadership and go-to-market/product commercialization work.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The mpd² curriculum is a cohort-based, highly sequenced program delivered in Fall 1 through Spring 2 terms, with 24 required courses plus a capstone project. Courses are five weeks long, the capstone runs during the final three quarters, and the same curriculum is offered in full-time and part-time pacing options.

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to AI in Product Development
core_courses.course_summary: Five-week, 0.5-credit course introducing the overall process of designing and developing products and services from opportunity identification to realization, with emphasis on emerging technology and AI systems.
learning_outcomes: Understand the overall product design and development process.
Identify opportunities and move concepts toward realization.
Apply AI and emerging technology in product development contexts.
Connect design decisions to product and service outcomes.

core_courses.course_summary: Five-week course on communication within corporate environments, including public speaking, live presentations, reports, emails, client and partner interaction, crisis communication, and difficult conversations.
learning_outcomes: Deliver effective public speaking and live presentations
Write clear reports and professional emails
Communicate effectively with clients and partners
Handle crisis communication in corporate settings
Navigate interpersonal and team difficult conversations

core_courses.course_title: Sustainability in the Product Development Process
core_courses.course_summary: Five-week required course (0.5 credits) on how product and service development choices affect performance, cost, and sustainability strategies.
learning_outcomes: Evaluate product and service development choices for sustainability impacts.
Relate sustainability strategies to performance and cost.
Apply sustainability thinking within the product development process.
Understand how development decisions shape environmental and business outcomes.

core_courses.course_summary: Examines corporate culture, team building, motivation, change management, feedback, mentoring, and cross-cultural issues.
Focuses on financial statements and managerial accounting topics used in product development decisions.
Emphasizes creative thinking for service design, including ethnography, empathy, brainstorming, storytelling, and innovation culture.
Covers valuation, capital structure, governance, risk management, financial modeling, and product cost analysis.
Teaches win-win negotiation, conflict resolution, ethics, planning, and objective standards in disputes.
Provides methods for leaders to drive decisions and actions through data analytics and visual methods.

core_courses.course_title: Global Product Design and Supply Chains
core_courses.course_summary: Addresses global product development challenges and the use of global supply and design chains across industries.
Defines the product manager role and develops skills for leading development, launch, and ongoing improvement.
Covers market research methods, questionnaire design, sampling, experimentation, and analytics for customer data.
A hands-on integration project focused on identifying unmet and under-met needs through research and team work.
Focuses on managing innovation and intellectual property, including patents, copyrights, trademarks, and strategy.
Covers planning, organizing, and creating a product innovation culture and roadmap.

core_courses.course_title: Creativity and Understanding through Design Sprints
core_courses.course_summary: Teaches iterative ideation, prototyping, user research, critique, and value proposition development through design sprints.

core_courses.course_summary: Examines leadership of product development teams, ethical leadership, talent development, and financial literacy.
Culminates the capstone project by integrating product development work into an iterative business model.

positioning.positioning_summary: Parsons’ MFA in Design and Technology is positioned as a studio-based, critical program at the intersection of creative computation, emerging technologies, and design practice. It emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration, research, and professional pathways in digital and interaction-focused fields, with explicit attention to social and ethical questions in technology.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MFA in Design Technology is a 60-credit, two-year program organized term-by-term across four semesters. Required study is front-loaded with major studio, computation, fabrication, and creative practice courses in year one, then thesis studio in year two, alongside recurring Cloud Salon sessions and advisor-guided electives that can be drawn from Parsons and other New School schools and colleges.

llm_rationales.note: Used Pratt's School of Information per-credit tuition of $1,894 and mandatory per-semester fees for a full-time student. For the 36-credit MS over 4 semesters, tuition is 36 x $1,894 = $68,184. Mandatory fees shown on the tuition page for full-time Fall/Spring are Academic Facilities $350 + Technology $305 + Student Health Services $200 + Student Activities $116 = $971 per semester; over 4 semesters that is $3,884. However, the program page indicates 36 credits and 2 years typically, but the cost comparison requested is tuition + mandatory program/academic fees only. Because the program is typically 4 semesters and the School of Information tuition page provides per-credit tuition, the total tuition+mandatory academic fees for the full program is $72,068 if all 36 credits are billed at the School of Information rate and full-time mandatory fees apply each semester.

Computed cross-program comparison cost in USD from tuition + mandatory program/academic fees only, excluding housing, board, insurance, and other non-mandatory or non-academic charges. Calculation used the School of Information per-credit tuition rate ($1,894) for 36 credits plus mandatory full-time Fall/Spring fees ($971 per semester x 4 semesters).

Confirmed that Pratt's Cost of Attendance page bundles tuition, required fees, and estimated cost of living, and explicitly says to add required fees, estimated cost of living, and year-one tuition for total estimated cost. Because this page does not isolate a tuition+mandatory-fees subtotal for the program, it was not used as the primary basis for the program-stated total beyond corroborating the tuition/fees scope.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MS in Information Experience Design is positioned as a professional, interdisciplinary UX program centered on digital interface and product design. Pratt describes it as a blend of information science, human-computer interaction, and user experience, preparing students for UX careers through research-based, human-centered design work and a portfolio-oriented curriculum.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Pratt’s Information Experience Design, MS is a 36-credit, typically 2-year graduate program in the School of Information. The curriculum is only partially specified on the fetched page: it names two required courses and presents a broader set of elective options, while also describing two areas of emphasis that let students shape a cohesive study plan toward design or research/evaluation careers. The program is organized around a digital portfolio and a user-centered, ethical, and practice-oriented UX curriculum.

core_courses.course_summary: Required School of Information course. Students learn usability principles and gain hands-on experience with common usability evaluation methods, including traditional user testing and inspection- and field-based methods. 3 credits.
learning_outcomes: Apply usability principles to digital interfaces.
Use user testing to evaluate design quality.
Use inspection-based methods to assess usability.
Use field-based methods to study real use contexts.

electives.summary: The program offers a wide variety of electives, and students may also shape their studies through two areas of emphasis: Design or Research and Evaluation. The Design emphasis recommends electives such as accessibility, service design, conversational UX, web development, visual communication, digital product design, mobile interaction, ethnography, speculative design, emotional design, information visualization, sustainable interaction design, UX design systems, extended reality, and advanced projects. The Research and Evaluation emphasis recommends electives such as human-information interaction, human-centered research methods, data analysis, content strategy, advanced usability, practical ethnography, community building, digital analytics, audience research, and advanced projects. The page frames these electives as supporting focused career pathways rather than a fixed elective rule set.

positioning.positioning_summary: A transdisciplinary, design-and-engineering master’s positioned around innovation, research, and real-world impact. The programme explicitly integrates design and engineering with science and enterprise, emphasizes collaboration across disciplines and industry, and highlights pathways into leadership, entrepreneurship, and research.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Innovation Design Engineering is a highly sequenced 360-credit, two-year full-time MA/MSc with a phased structure across six terms. The curriculum combines required core units in Phase 1 foundations, Phase 2 consolidation, and Phase 3 mastery, with an elective module in the final phase to support specialism development. The programme is jointly run with Imperial College London and emphasizes transdisciplinary design, engineering, science, and enterprise.

Evaluate how work affects people and how people affect work
Treat the person as a system component
Apply human factors concepts to system evaluation

core_courses.course_summary: Required 3-unit core course in the HF/E sequence. Focuses on hypotheses testing, regression, ANOVA, factorial and block designs, nonparametric methods, power, sample size, and factor analysis.
learning_outcomes: Test hypotheses using appropriate statistical methods
Apply linear regression and analysis of variance
Design factorial and block experiments
Use nonparametric methods and multiple comparisons
Estimate power and sample size for experiments

core_courses.course_summary: Required 2-unit professional seminar for HF/E students, typically completed in the first year. Covers public presentation, terminology, field evaluations, current research, and industry issues in human factors and ergonomics.
learning_outcomes: Present human factors ideas professionally
Use core HF/E terminology accurately
Discuss current research and industry issues
Conduct or interpret field evaluations
Prepare for professional HF/Ergo practice

core_courses.course_summary: Psychology seminar course required in the core curriculum.
Kinesiology core course focused on motor learning.
Required human factors experiments and writing requirement.

electives.summary: The program requires 9 elective units. Electives are chosen from a wide range of graduate courses on the SJSU campus, especially in Industrial Engineering, Psychology, Kinesiology, and other departments, and the Program Director or Graduate Advisor must approve the plan. The program also publishes a potential list of approved electives spanning human factors, HCI, ergonomics, cognition, biomechanics, safety, research, and related topics, which supports students who want to tailor the degree toward design-focused or research-oriented interests.

llm_rationales.note: The application page explicitly names the program as MFA Interaction Design, asks applicants about what excites them about interaction design, and says the department seeks candidates excited to shape the future of design and technology.

The page says the program aims to make products, services, and experiences more inclusive, sustainable, and joyful, and the statement prompt asks about designing systems, communities, or experiences, supporting a social-impact orientation.

The program is described as full-time and studio-based, with portfolio requirements covering design work, usability and social science research, strategy and writing, coding, and project management, indicating a professional practice orientation.
The curriculum page states there is 'a matriculation of two academic years' and that students must complete 60 credits, which supports a four-semester duration estimate.
The program overview describes the MFA Interaction Design as a 'two-year graduate program,' aligning with the four-semester duration category.

Used SVA's 2026-27 graduate tuition for Interaction Design ($30,845 per semester for Fall/Spring) plus the Interaction Design department fee ($400 per semester). The program is two academic years / four semesters, so tuition and mandatory program/academic fees total 4 x ($30,845 + $400) = $122,980 USD. Health insurance, housing, and other non-mandatory charges were excluded.

Used the program-stated tuition per semester and department fee for the 2026-27 Interaction Design graduate program. This is a tuition + mandatory department fee subtotal only, not housing or full cost of attendance. Computed program total is $122,980 USD across the two-year curriculum.
Confirmed the program length is a two-year graduate program with a two academic year residency and 60 credits, supporting multiplication of the per-semester tuition and fee across four semesters.
The program states degree candidates must complete 60 credits, so the curriculum uses semester credit hours.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MFA Interaction Design program is positioned as a studio-based, professional design program centered on interaction design and digital product/service/experience work, with strong emphasis on inclusive, sustainable, and socially responsive design. The application materials also frame it as welcoming interdisciplinary applicants and those interested in shaping the future of design and technology.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MFA Interaction Design is a two-year, 60-credit program with a strongly prescribed first-year/second-year progression. The curriculum emphasizes interaction design, human-centered research, prototyping, inclusive design, business acumen, and a final thesis project approved by the thesis committee and department chair. No formal concentrations or tracks are listed.

core_courses.course_summary: The MFA Interaction Design program is a full-time, studio-based graduate program focused on shaping the future of design and technology through inclusive, sustainable, and joyful experiences. Applicants are asked to show interest in interaction design, systems, communities, and experiences they want to design.
learning_outcomes: Understand how interaction design shapes systems, communities, and experiences.
Develop a design practice grounded in values, questions, and future vision.
Communicate design motivations and process clearly in written and video form.
Demonstrate a point of view through a curated portfolio of projects.
Engage with inclusive, sustainable, and joyful design challenges.

core_courses.course_summary: A required full-year inclusive design course emphasizing co-design, rigorous research, ethics, accessibility, and designing with people rather than for them.
learning_outcomes: Practice co-design with people, not on their behalf
Evaluate and challenge biases and positionality during design
Conduct rigorous, critical, and ethical research
Design inclusive experiences that address accessibility and inequality
Avoid jumping to solutions before understanding people and problems

core_courses.course_summary: 3 credits. Students learn service design as a way to craft the total context, conditions, and cultures of experiences. The course teaches service design tools to identify opportunities, frame problem spaces, develop innovative directions, and communicate solutions.
learning_outcomes: Understand when and where service design is applicable
Use service design tools to identify opportunities
Frame problem spaces for service experiences
Develop and communicate innovative service directions
Consider the broader context of people, businesses, and systems

core_courses.course_summary: The program identifies design research as a central subject area for understanding people, needs, and experience.

Information architecture is named as a central subject area in the program’s curriculum.
Ubiquitous computing is identified as a central subject area in the curriculum.

In the final semester, each student completes a thesis project that must be reviewed and approved for degree conferral.

Curriculum emphasizes design-based methods, business tools, ethically grounded professional leadership, and projects for diverse stakeholders worldwide, reinforcing interdisciplinary, professional, and social impact themes.

Used the Parsons School of Design graduate tuition table for MS Strategic Design and Management: tuition is $2,182 per credit and the program is 36 credits. Computed tuition as 36 x $2,182 = $78,552. Added mandatory university fees supported on the same page: University Services $720 per semester x 4 semesters = $2,880, Student Senate $9 per semester x 4 = $36, and Orientation $177 once = $177. Excluded housing, meals, insurance, and other COA items. Total tuition + mandatory program/academic fees used for cross-program comparison = $81,645 USD.

positioning.positioning_summary: Parsons’ Strategic Design and Management MS is positioned as a design-led, interdisciplinary professional degree that blends design thinking with management, applied social sciences, and business tools. The program emphasizes real-world, studio-based work, leadership, entrepreneurship, and social/environmental challenge-solving, with a STEM-designated and globally oriented format.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MS in Strategic Design and Management is a 36-credit, two-year program organized as a progressively sequenced four-semester plan. Required coursework is arranged by term, with three 3-credit courses each semester, and the remaining credits are taken as advisor-guided electives. The curriculum combines design-based methods, business strategy, ethics, and studio/capstone work, and the program is described as available on campus, online, or in mixed format.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MAT curriculum is an interdisciplinary graduate program built around a common first-year foundation, area-focused electives, seminars, projects, and culminating thesis/project or dissertation work. Evidence shows three emphasis areas—electronic music and sound design, visual and spatial arts, and multimedia engineering—and a flexible but structured path with required core courses, electives, and supervised research.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MS in Creative Technology & Design is positioned as a multidisciplinary, professionally oriented program that blends engineering, design, and technical know-how to prepare students for real-world design challenges. The evidence also emphasizes project-based portfolio building, flexibility across application areas like UX/UI, product development, interactive media, and game design, and opportunities to engage with research labs and innovation-focused work.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program is a 30-credit master’s degree with multiple tracks. The Reading Instruction and Literacy Leadership track has a fixed set of seven 3-credit required courses plus 9 elective credits. The STEM Education track has required area-based course choices, a 3-credit capstone seminar, 9 additional School of Education credits, and up to 6 credits of approved outside content courses; the materials describe the track as 30 credits total. The curriculum is therefore structured but track-dependent, with some required courses named and some requirements expressed as credit-area choices rather than a single fixed course list.

electives.summary: Electives are track-specific and advisor-guided. In the Reading Instruction and Literacy Leadership track, students choose 9 additional credit hours of electives in consultation with an advisor, and the page says education electives must be at the 5000 level or above. In the STEM Education track, students choose at least 3 credits in Learning and Development, at least 3 credits in Foundations of Education Curriculum, at least 12 credits in Curriculum and Instruction in Mathematics and Science, and at least 9 additional School of Education credits, with up to 6 credits of approved Arts & Sciences content courses allowed; the track also requires one 3-credit seminar capstone. The electives support specialization by letting students deepen either literacy leadership or STEM education.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MFA in Design for Responsible Innovation is positioned as a three-year, research-oriented design program that prepares students to create inclusive futures across academia, industry, and communities. Its emphasis on responsible innovation, equity, sustainability, ethics, social justice, and design research supports a social-impact and research-forward profile, with clear ties to interdisciplinary practice and digital/interaction-oriented design methods.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MFA in Design for Responsible Innovation is a 64-credit, three-year graduate program with a clear graduate course sequence and thesis requirement. The curriculum centers on ARTD 451, 551, 570, 595, and 599, with additional electives; students without an undergraduate design background are directed into design studio electives in the first graduate year. The evidence shows a structured progression from research and methodology into studio work and thesis, but not a full semester-by-semester plan for every required element.

identity.credential_name: Master of Fine Arts in Art and Design, Industrial Design Concentration

positioning.positioning_summary: The program is positioned as a professional, research-informed industrial design degree that emphasizes interdisciplinary work, human-centered and ethically produced design, and strong connections to social impact and emerging practice areas such as design thinking, interfaces, interactions, and user experiences.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Industrial Design MFA/MDes curriculum is a structured 64-credit program built around a fixed six-course graduate sequence (ARTD 501–506) that moves from inquiry and ideation through implementation and thesis project development, followed by ARTD 599 thesis registration/completion. Students also complete approved design studio electives, an academic elective, and additional electives including seminars. The evidence describes a studio-based, research-informed curriculum with a strong thesis component and some flexibility in elective selection, but no distinct tracks within the Industrial Design degree itself.

core_courses.course_title: Industrial Design I: From Inquiry to Ideation
core_courses.course_summary: 6-credit core studio that begins with benchmarking and focuses on early design thinking, research, analysis, user needs, ideation, and creative thinking strategies.
learning_outcomes: Use benchmarking to assess current design skill level
Apply research and analysis to identify user needs
Generate ideation in response to user needs
Use creative thinking strategies in early design work
Develop foundational design thinking for later studio courses

core_courses.course_title: Industrial Design II: From Ideation to Implementation
core_courses.course_summary: 6-credit core studio course that builds on ARTD 501 by generating ideas and concepts from user needs and developing them into resolved design proposals, with attention to user feedback, prototyping, responsible and sustainable manufacturing, and communication with users and stakeholders.
learning_outcomes: Generate ideas and concepts from identified user needs.
Develop resolved design proposals from earlier research insights.
Use user feedback to refine design directions.
Consider responsible and sustainable manufacturing.
Communicate design solutions to users and stakeholders.

core_courses.course_title: Industrial Design III: Design Project Formulation
core_courses.course_summary: 6-credit graduate studio course that integrates research and design process in a holistic project, rehearsing thesis investigations and helping establish a personal direction for the student's work.
learning_outcomes: Integrate research and design process in a holistic project.
Rehearse thesis investigations through project formulation.
Establish a personal direction for design work.
Apply user-centered design thinking to project development.

core_courses.course_summary: Covers supervised research activities for the master’s thesis design project under a thesis committee.
Focuses on ideation and prototyping for the thesis design project as the second stage of the thesis sequence.

core_courses.course_title: Industrial Design VI: Thesis Project Implementation
core_courses.course_summary: Final studio course in the thesis project sequence, culminating in a public exhibition of thesis work.
Thesis registration and document-production course providing faculty guidance in writing and depositing the thesis for the graduate degree.

electives.summary: Electives are partly structured rather than fully open: the program requires approved design studio electives from Art and Design, Architecture, or Engineering, plus an academic elective and additional electives that may include seminars. The evidence says students develop a plan of study with the graduate coordinator, and some elective credits are approved by that coordinator; for the MFA DRI, studio electives and free electives are drawn from across the School, the college, and the wider campus. These electives support individualized study and, in the DRI context, can align with the student’s research direction, but the Industrial Design degree itself does not describe formal tracks.

positioning.positioning_summary: MHCI+D is positioned as an intensive, year-long, interdisciplinary program at the intersection of human-computer interaction, design, technology, and research, with strong emphasis on studio-based collaboration, prototyping, and career preparation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MHCI+D is a one-year, quarter-structured, cohort-based program with a pre-Autumn immersion, a fixed sequence of required core studios and lectures across Autumn, Winter, Spring, and Summer, and a three-course capstone sequence. Electives are typically 2–3 courses drawn mainly from graduate offerings across campus, especially the sponsoring departments, and they support deeper exploration rather than formal tracks.

core_courses.course_summary: A five-day pre-Autumn intensive workshop that orients new students to core HCI and design ideas, terminology, and studio skills. 2 quarter credits.

A Summer final studio in which teams continue iterative design, prototyping, and evaluation on a comprehensive capstone project.

positioning.positioning_summary: CCA’s MDes in Interaction Design is positioned as an intensive, STEM-designated interaction design program that combines systems thinking, human-centered technology, and experience design with a strong social-impact orientation. The program emphasizes real-world, community-engaged work through Social Lab, while also signaling professional preparation for in-demand design careers and a Bay Area technology context.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: CCA’s MDes in Interaction Design is a one-year, three-semester, 36-unit program with a prescribed sequence of studio and seminar courses. The curriculum begins with foundations, research, form, and communication, then moves into prototyping, systems, and a Social Lab, and concludes with leadership, story, experience, and a second Social Lab. One graduate-wide elective is included in the spring term.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: CCA’s MBA in Design Strategy is described as a two-year, low-residency program organized by semester. The evidence does not provide a formal course catalog or credit total, but it does show a recurring structure of core business courses paired with studios across design, business strategy, and social innovation, plus electives and capstone projects. The curriculum emphasizes design thinking, prototyping, market insight, leadership, and real-client project work, with example focus areas including circular economy strategy, AI ethics, sustainable business models, and leadership for creative organizations.

electives.summary: The program appears to use a flexible elective-and-capstone structure rather than fixed named concentrations. The materials also describe semester-based studio work and core business courses, but they do not specify a formal elective rule set, outside-department limits, or a published list of approved electives.

identity.program_name: Art, Culture, and Technology (ACT) Graduate Program
identity.credential_name: Master of Science in Art, Culture, and Technology (SMACT)

positioning.positioning_summary: MIT’s Art, Culture, and Technology graduate program is positioned as a highly selective, two-year, research-based and transdisciplinary Master of Science centered on critical art practice, theory, and discourse. It is housed within MIT’s Department of Architecture / School of Architecture and Planning, and explicitly bridges art with culture, technology, science, design, and embedded research.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The SMACT curriculum is a two-year, academic-year-only graduate program totaling 135 units, centered on a required studio/thesis colloquium and a staged thesis sequence, with additional restricted ACT electives and open electives. Students progress through thesis preparation and tutorial work while also selecting from a defined pool of ACT subjects in media, studio, seminar, and workshop areas.

core_courses.course_title: Art, Culture, and Technology Studio and Thesis Colloquium
core_courses.course_summary: Restricted to SMACT students and serving as the core of the curriculum, this 4.THG-linked colloquium combines studio/project development with thesis preparation, regular presentations, critiques, and required midterm and final reviews. Students are expected to present evolving thesis work and attend ACT events.
learning_outcomes: Develop and present evolving thesis-related project work regularly.
Apply artistic practice as critical inquiry and knowledge dissemination.
Use methods of investigation, documentation, and display in project development.
Respond to critiques, reviews, and faculty feedback.
Prepare thesis work through interdisciplinary theory and criticism.

core_courses.course_title: Thesis I: Art, Culture, and Technology Theory and Criticism Colloquium
core_courses.course_summary: Required first thesis-sequence colloquium introducing foundational texts in contemporary theory and criticism at the intersection of art, culture, and technology.
learning_outcomes: Engage foundational texts in contemporary theory and criticism.
Apply methodological perspectives to interdisciplinary artistic practice.
Develop a thesis statement and revised thesis direction.
Produce an annotated bibliography and comparative short papers.
Situate work within art, culture, and technology discourse.

core_courses.course_summary: Second thesis-sequence subject focused on selecting a thesis topic, developing an approach and proposal, and preparing the thesis outline and supporting materials. The course is listed as 3 units and restricted to first-year SMACT students.
learning_outcomes: Select a viable thesis topic
Develop a thesis approach and method
Prepare a thesis proposal and outline
Use artist writings and academic standards
Refine work through peer review and faculty conferences

core_courses.course_summary: Third thesis-sequence subject supporting independent thesis research and development through tutorials, critiques, research strategy guidance, and written presentation support.

electives.summary: The degree includes 21 units of restricted ACT electives drawn from a named pool of ACT studio, seminar, workshop, and media subjects, plus 18 units of open electives. The catalog also describes an additional 18 units of graduate-level subjects approved by the advisor, indicating that elective choice is structured but still broad and can support students’ individual interests rather than formal tracks.

positioning.positioning_summary: MIT SDM is positioned as a professional, interdisciplinary engineering-and-management master’s for early- and mid-career professionals, centered on solving complex sociotechnical problems and preparing technically grounded leaders. The curriculum is highly customizable and explicitly spans MIT School of Engineering and MIT Sloan, with systems thinking, systems engineering, product design, leadership, and management skills emphasized.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIT’s SDM master’s curriculum is a unit-based, highly customizable program built around a required three-course foundation sequence in systems design and management plus a required thesis. Students then complete approved depth/foundation coursework and restricted electives drawn from engineering and management subject lists, with some split-credit subjects allowed. The evidence shows a clear core sequence and category-based elective structure, but not a full term-by-term degree map.

core_courses.course_title: Foundations of System Design and Management I
core_courses.course_summary: Presents the foundations of systems architecture, systems engineering, and project management in an integrated format through discussion, guest speakers, team projects, and individual assignments. The core runs across the fall and spring semesters and IAP as a three-term sequence.
learning_outcomes: Understand systems architecture, systems engineering, and project management foundations.
Apply systems thinking to complex technical and managerial problems.
Use stakeholder analysis, planning, requirements, and integration methods.
Evaluate verification, validation, safety, risk, and organizational design issues.
Work effectively in teams on integrated design projects.

core_courses.course_title: Foundations of System Design and Management II
core_courses.course_summary: Deepens systems architecture, systems engineering, and project management through lectures, recitations, guest speakers, and team projects. Emphasizes the transition from early conceptual design to detailed design and system integration, and includes a technology showcase and project forum for selecting EM.413 projects.
learning_outcomes: Deepen understanding of systems architecture and systems engineering
Apply project management methods in team-based settings
Move from conceptual design to detailed design and integration
Evaluate and select projects for EM.413
Collaborate with sponsors, faculty, and peers on design challenges

core_courses.course_title: Foundations of System Design and Management III
core_courses.course_summary: Advanced core course in systems architecture, systems engineering, and project management. Uses lectures, recitations, guest lectures, and a semester-long team project to address complexity management, systems integration, verification and validation, lifecycle management, and global portfolio/program management.
learning_outcomes: Apply systems architecture and systems engineering concepts to complex problems.
Manage integration, verification, validation, and lifecycle concerns.
Address quality, safety, robustness, resilience, and evolvability.
Work effectively on a semester-long team project.
Analyze portfolios and programs in a global context.

core_courses.course_summary: Required thesis research leading to the SM thesis, arranged with an appropriate MIT faculty member.

electives.summary: Electives are not open-ended; students choose from approved and restricted subject lists in management and engineering, with additional split-credit subjects that can count across both areas. The curriculum groups options into thematic management areas and engineering departments, and the evidence indicates that these electives support customization rather than distinct formal tracks.

llm_rationales.note: Catalog overview says MAS 'focuses on the invention, study, and creative use of new technologies' and draws on computer science, cognitive sciences, communications, design, and the expressive arts; it also states academic programs are 'intimately linked' with Media Lab research programs.

Course list shows hands-on technical and making-oriented subjects such as How to Make (Almost) Anything, Decoders 1.9: Introduction to Microfabrication, Tangible Interfaces, Principles of Neuroengineering, and Affective Computing and Multimodal Interaction.

Evidence uses MIT-style unit counts and subject unit formats such as 66 graduate academic course units, 12 units per semester minimum, and subject notations like 3-0-9 and 0-12-0, so the curriculum is best treated as other_or_mixed rather than a single standard credit system.

Doctoral requirements are explicitly year-sequenced (MAS.921 in year 1, MAS.945 in year 2, MAS.950 in year 3, dissertation thereafter), while master’s requirements include thesis prep and thesis milestones but many other offerings are term-flexible; this supports partially_sequenced.

The evidence does not describe distinct tracks, concentrations, or specializations chosen among by students; it describes a broad interdisciplinary program with many topical offerings, so offers_specialization is false.

positioning.positioning_summary: MIT Media Arts and Sciences is positioned as an interdisciplinary, research-intensive program at the intersection of computing, design, communications, learning, robotics, and other technical fields, with strong emphasis on emerging technologies, human-computer interaction, and social impact. The program also highlights hands-on making and fabrication through courses in digital fabrication, microfabrication, tangible interfaces, and related labs.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Media Arts and Sciences curriculum is a mixed graduate-and-undergraduate program with a strong project, research, and thesis orientation. Evidence shows a fixed doctoral sequence (proseminar, general exam, dissertation proposal, dissertation) and a master’s sequence with thesis preparation and a thesis, but the broader course menu is flexible and term-based rather than a single rigid path. The program also publishes rotating semester course lists and many special topics, indicating substantial elective choice within a research-intensive, interdisciplinary environment.

Orientation-style special subject limited to MAS students; the source lists it as an introductory course for the program.

electives.summary: The program does not publish a single fixed elective rule on the fetched pages, but the degree charts and course lists show a broad elective space made up of regular MAS subjects, special topics, and approved substitutions. For the CMS undergraduate major, electives are structured as six subjects with one media practice subject, one additional CI-M subject, and a set of restricted electives; for MAS graduate study, the pages emphasize term-by-term offerings, independent study, special subjects, and thesis-related work rather than a single closed elective list.

The curriculum is laid out term-by-term across Fall, Spring, and Summer with a prescribed sequence of required courses and format-specific applied learning/internship components, so it is highly sequenced.

positioning.positioning_summary: The ETC admissions page positions the program as an interdisciplinary, professionally oriented entertainment technology degree that blends creative and technical work. It explicitly notes that applicants may submit either a portfolio or GRE scores, that the curriculum is almost entirely team-based, and that portfolios can include traditional or digital artwork, graphic design, writing, coding, photos, and videos. The page also emphasizes career goals, project work each semester, and merit scholarships.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Entertainment Technology Center master’s curriculum is a two-year, semester-based, interdisciplinary program built around an Immersion Semester followed by three project-heavy semesters. Students begin with four required foundational courses, then move into recurring team projects and electives, including approved concentration-specific electives for the Transformational Play option.

electives.summary: After the first semester, students take electives each term and may choose courses within ETC or in other CMU schools and departments. The elective mix is broad and project-based, helping students explore interests such as UX, product design, location-based entertainment, animation, level design, VR design, and other areas; the Transformational Play concentration further restricts students to approved concentration electives.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MHCI is positioned as a professional, interdisciplinary HCI program centered on human-computer interaction and UX/user-centered research. It explicitly emphasizes preparing students for industry careers, combines design, computer science, social science, and learning science perspectives, and highlights a substantial external-client capstone; it is also designated a STEM program.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MHCI is a three-semester professional program completed from August to August. The first semester is a required core of HCI methods and interaction design courses, followed by a spring term with advanced design and the first project course, and a summer term with the capstone project. Students also complete four electives, which may be drawn broadly across Carnegie Mellon and can be used to broaden or focus study.

electives.summary: MHCI requires four electives, each normally a full-semester course worth 9–12 units; two mini-courses can count together if they total at least 9 units. Electives may come from HCII or across Carnegie Mellon, and students often use them either to broaden their interdisciplinary background or to focus on a particular interest area. Outside-HCII electives require approval from the MHCI Director of Student Affairs.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MID is positioned as a human-centered, interdisciplinary design program with strong emphasis on research, technology, and professional practice. Its page explicitly frames the degree as a Human-Centered Technology Design STEM program, highlights collaboration across disciplines, and centers interaction design, healthcare design, service design, product design, and user-experience research.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MID is a two-year, 48-credit program built from required core courses, at least 16 credits of approved graduate electives, and a 12-credit thesis or non-thesis project. Core courses are sequenced in a recommended order, with Graduate Studio I followed by Graduate Studio II and Intro to Thesis Studies before the final thesis/project work. Electives are flexible and may come from MID offerings or other Georgia Tech departments, and they can support student specialization.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MS in Digital Media is positioned as an arts-and-humanities-based graduate program in digital media design and critique, with a strong studio/seminar and research component. Its framing emphasizes interdisciplinary work across media, design, technology, and cultural analysis, while also highlighting professional preparation for leadership roles in a changing digital culture.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MS in Digital Media is a 36-credit, studio- and seminar-based program centered on digital media design, critique, making, labs, and research. The published degree plan shows a prescribed first-year sequence with required core courses, a repeated Project Studio component, 15 credits of electives, and a final choice between a project or thesis, plus a non-credit summer internship.

Analyze the interplay between digital technology and culture.

positioning.positioning_summary: Georgia Tech’s MS-HCI is positioned as an interdisciplinary, professionally oriented HCI/UX program that blends practical skills with theoretical knowledge, emphasizes design/research/implementation/evaluation of human-computer interfaces, and includes a required summer internship and capstone project.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Georgia Tech’s MS-HCI is a 36-credit, four-semester interdisciplinary program with a common core, specialization coursework tied to the student’s admitted unit/track, electives, a required summer internship, and a 6-credit master’s project. The curriculum is partly sequenced because the core is fixed and some courses have term placement, but many specialization and elective choices are flexible within track rules.

core_courses.course_summary: Fixed first-semester core course in HCI foundations. The course covers evidence-based design, user-centered and universal design, and hands-on interface design projects.
learning_outcomes: Understand human-computer interface importance in design and development
Apply evidence-based, user-centered, and universal design approaches
Use concepts and strategies to make interface design decisions
Learn tools, techniques, and ideas for interface design
Communicate effectively across psychology, design, and computer science

core_courses.course_summary: A required 4-credit core course in psychological research methods for HCI, taken in the first semester alongside HCI Foundations.
learning_outcomes: Apply research methods to HCI problems
Analyze user tasks, needs, and behavior
Evaluate interfaces using evidence-based methods
Understand perceptual, cognitive, and social factors
Support design decisions with research evidence

core_courses.course_summary: Required professional preparation and practice core course for MS-HCI, taken as one credit hour in the first-year fall and one in the second-year fall.
learning_outcomes: Prepare for professional practice in HCI and UX
Develop practical skills for industry readiness
Strengthen theoretical understanding of HCI work
Support design, implementation, and evaluation of interfaces
Build professional preparation across the program timeline

core_courses.course_summary: A required 6-credit culminating master’s project completed under faculty supervision, typically during the last two semesters.

electives.summary: Electives are structured by specialization and by track rules rather than being fully open. Students choose specialization courses within their admitted area, and the catalog also allows general electives from a broad set of approved departments across campus. Some tracks require a minimum amount of non-track elective credit, and up to 3 credits of Special Problems in HCI may count toward electives; electives support both breadth and the student’s chosen specialization.

llm_rationales.note: Selected interaction_design because the program is explicitly labeled 'MDES | Interaction' and includes multiple interaction-oriented studio/research courses. Selected social_impact because courses reference human behavior, social constructs, environmental imperatives, community engagement, and social-based concerns.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MDES | Interaction curriculum is positioned as a research-driven, studio-based design program centered on interaction design practice. Its coursework emphasizes design studios, design research methods, contemporary critical dialogue, thesis development, and independent project work, with explicit attention to human behavior, social constructs, environmental imperatives, and communication of research and design outcomes. The program also signals broader social-impact and interdisciplinary orientation through community engagement, collaborative projects, and the university’s stated mission around practice-based art + design and research that addresses complex challenges.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MDes Interaction is a two-year, on-campus, 60-credit program organized across five terms. The curriculum is highly sequenced and centers on a required progression of design studio, research methods, contemporary dialogues, directed studies, thesis project seminars, and final thesis work, with milestones such as Advancement Review, Performance + Progress Review, Thesis Symposia, and final presentation. The program emphasizes interaction design, UX/UI, emerging technologies, research, critique, and thesis development rather than elective specialization.

core_courses.course_summary: Introductory graduate studio focused on tangible design processes, sketching, visualization, critique, and research-informed practice. 6 credits, letter grade.

Critique and communicate design insights to diverse audiences
Integrate research findings into studio work

core_courses.course_summary: Seminar on critical and theoretical contexts for contemporary design, using readings, media phenomena, case studies, presentations, writing, and debate to examine social, economic, technical, environmental, and aesthetic issues.

Engage debates through readings, presentations, writing, and discussion.
