positioning.positioning_summary: A research- and practice-based, full-time interdisciplinary MDes centered on studio practice, design research, and an independent thesis project, with strong emphasis on human-centered design, community/sustainability, and access to studios, labs, and research facilities.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MDes (Interdisciplinary) is a 5-term, 60-credit, full-residency on-campus program with a fixed progression from studio, research methods, and contemporary dialogues into directed studies, independent project work, and thesis seminars. The curriculum is explicitly term-mapped and culminates in thesis development and final symposia, with no elective structure or specialization tracks described in the fetched material.

core_courses.course_summary: 6-credit introductory graduate studio focused on tangible design processes, sketching, visualization, prototyping, scenarios, and storyboarding to explore and synthesize design ideas.
learning_outcomes: Discuss, explore, and synthesize design through studio practice.
Use sketching and visualization to embody research insights.
Apply metaphoric and narrative methods such as video prototyping and storyboarding.
Communicate design ideas effectively to diverse audiences.
Respond to critiques on visualizing and communicating design work.

core_courses.course_summary: Introduces graduate-level design methodologies. Students develop research for individual projects and demonstrate bibliographic, methodological, and theoretical project work.
learning_outcomes: Develop research for individual design projects

Navigate methods engaging human behavior and social contexts
Communicate a project work plan and thesis-related methods

core_courses.course_summary: Seminar on critical and theoretical contexts for contemporary design, using readings, presentations, writing assignments, discussions, and case debates.
learning_outcomes: Examine critical and theoretical contexts for contemporary design.

Analyze media phenomena, literature, and case studies.
Participate in readings, presentations, writing, and discussions.
Demonstrate leadership in debates on design issues.

core_courses.course_summary: Second studio course that advances thesis direction through exploration, ideation, experimentation, and critique.

Self-directed module/studio that supports thesis refinement and direction prior to candidacy review.
Advanced independent studio work focused on finalizing the thesis project and methods of communication.

electives.summary: The curriculum is organized into Foundation, Elective(1), Elective(2), and Common sections. Elective(1) contains project and thesis options, while Elective(2) offers specialized design-engineering topics such as IoT product development, prototyping, sustainability, mechatronics, smart home devices, materials, and product-market fit. identity.credential_name: cand. des / Master of Arts (MA) in Design - Strategic

positioning.positioning_summary: The programme is positioned as a research-led, interdisciplinary design/architecture master that emphasizes collaboration across disciplines, real-world projects with companies and organizations, and strategic design thinking for innovation and entrepreneurship. It also foregrounds sustainability, social and societal change, and professional practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Strategic Design and Entrepreneurship master’s programme is a four-semester, project-based curriculum taught in English and organized around a fixed sequence of semester modules. It combines Royal Danish Academy and CBS teaching, with collaborative design, entrepreneurship, law/ethics, consulting, and a final master’s project. The programme is explicitly interdisciplinary and specialization-oriented, but the evidence does not show distinct student-chosen tracks within this programme.

core_courses.course_summary: First-semester collaborative project module taught by the Royal Danish Academy for all students. The programme is project-based, interdisciplinary, and uses design thinking methods to address real-world challenges with companies and organisations.

Apply design thinking methods to innovation and entrepreneurship.
Develop concrete design proposals for real-world challenges.
Use user and stakeholder facilitation in project work.
Explain design process and role in cross-disciplinary collaboration.

core_courses.course_summary: First-semester Royal Danish Academy module for all students, focused on collaborative design processes, interdisciplinary teamwork, and design thinking methods for innovation and entrepreneurship. 15 ECTS.

Develop user- and stakeholder-facilitation skills
Create concrete design proposals for real-world challenges
Explain design processes in cross-disciplinary collaboration

electives.summary: The evidence does not describe a separate elective system for this programme. positioning.positioning_summary: The program is positioned as an interaction design master’s that combines practical and scientific research methods with innovative project work using a range of technologies. Its framing emphasizes interaction design itself, research, digital and technological practice, and a socially aware orientation through human-centered design and projects engaging ecological or public-interest contexts.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MA Design, Interaction Design at ZHdK is a four-semester, 120-ECTS program with a clearly prescribed semester-by-semester sequence of required major courses. The curriculum combines studios, interaction research, theory, design research methods, conferences, a study trip, media production/workshop introduction, thesis development, and the master’s thesis. The evidence does not show elective choice within the major curriculum, though the broader ZHdK major-minor model allows minors outside the major.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MA Design, Industrial Design is a full-time, four-semester, 90-credit program with a clearly structured progression from first-year collaborative and individual projects into second-year thesis development, mentoring, a graduation project, dissertation work, and exhibition presentation. The curriculum includes recurring interdisciplinary modules and a cross-disciplinary minor component that occupies about a quarter of course time and offers thematic elective directions, but the sources do not provide course codes or credit values for individual modules beyond the overall program scope.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MA Design, Knowledge Visualization is a four-semester, research-oriented master’s program built around an individual thesis/project. The evidence shows a structured progression, but most module areas are described thematically rather than with term-by-term course placement.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master of Design in Smart Service Design requires 37 credits total, delivered in a mixed-mode format over 1 year full-time or 3 years part-time. The curriculum is staged: it begins with Research and Analysis for Design, moves through elective study, then specialism study in studios and lab studies, and ends with Research and Academic Writing plus a capstone or industry research project. Electives are organized into two pools, with 12 credits from Pool A and 3 credits from Pool B, allowing students to tailor their studies within the program framework.

core_courses.course_summary: Compulsory common subject introducing research and analysis for design within service design practice, with emphasis on human-centered, systemic, and socio-technical perspectives.
learning_outcomes: Understand end-to-end service journeys and service ecosystems.
Apply human-centered design to stakeholder needs and contexts.
Use research and analysis methods for service design.
Develop service design strategies for business and societal challenges.
Work with studio, workshop, and industry research project formats.

core_courses.course_summary: Compulsory common subject focused on academic writing and research communication in the Smart Service Design programme.
learning_outcomes: Write academically for design research contexts
Communicate research findings clearly and persuasively
Document and structure design-related arguments
Prepare scholarly writing for the capstone and programme work

core_courses.course_title: Advanced Service Design Methods and Tools
core_courses.course_summary: A compulsory specialism major subject in Smart Service Design focused on advanced service design methodologies, tools, and systems thinking for service innovation.
learning_outcomes: Apply advanced service design methodologies
Use tools for service design development
Analyze service ecosystems and contexts
Transform opportunities into service experiences
Work with systems thinking approaches

core_courses.course_summary: A compulsory specialism major subject examining socio-technical service ecosystems and their design implications.

core_courses.course_title: Systemic Innovation and Designing Future Services
core_courses.course_summary: A compulsory specialism major subject focused on systemic innovation and future service design.

core_courses.course_title: Lab Studies in Data-Driven Service Design
core_courses.course_summary: A compulsory lab studies subject applying data-driven approaches to service design in a research-oriented setting.
A compulsory specialism studio where students develop an integrative Smart Service Design project.
The culminating capstone research project that synthesizes learning in a real-world service design context.

core_courses.course_title: Academic Integrity and Ethics in Science
core_courses.course_summary: A required 1-credit academic integrity and ethics subject associated with the degree requirements.

electives.summary: Electives are structured into two pools rather than being fully open-ended. Pool A contributes 12 credits and draws from foundation subjects and specialism major subjects, while Pool B contributes 3 credits and includes interdisciplinary studio/project subjects and lab studies subjects. The elective structure lets students customize their learning profile and supports specialization within the broader Smart Service Design curriculum.

llm_rationales.note: Program positioning emphasizes design thinking, sustainable futures, wicked sociotechnical problems, interdisciplinary collaboration, cultural learning in China, and professional pathways in business, public service, and nonprofit contexts.
The program is described as a two-year full-time master program requiring four semesters, with a maximum study time of four academic years.
The uploaded brochure does not provide tuition or mandatory fee amounts; cost fields are therefore left null pending source verification.
The brochure provides a 55-credit curriculum organized by year and semester, including seminars, studios, technology-enabled design, thesis work, field practice, cultural studies, and electives.

positioning.positioning_summary: Tongji University’s English-speaking MDes in Design Thinking and Sustainable Future is a two-year international program focused on cultivating creative leadership for complex sociotechnical challenges. It frames design thinking as a method for addressing wicked problems across social, organizational, individual, and environmental contexts, while combining studio work, technology-enabled design, ethics, future scenarios, thesis research, co-op practice, and Chinese cultural study.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program requires 55 Tongji Credits over a structured two-year sequence. Year one establishes a foundation in design thinking, storytelling, action-oriented studio practice, civilization, future scenarios, and ethics. Year two develops individual professional and research directions through technology-enabled design, a specialized studio elective, research writing, thesis proposal, thesis project, field practice or co-op, cultural studies, Chinese language, lecture series, and additional electives.

core_courses.course_summary: Introduces design thinking as a framework for interpreting and addressing complex individual, organizational, social, and environmental challenges.
A first-year studio emphasizing action-oriented design practice and the application of design thinking methods.
Develops visual communication and storytelling capacities for design work.
Examines design in relation to civilization, culture, and broader social contexts.
A future-oriented studio focused on scenario development and long-term sociotechnical change.
Addresses ethical considerations in design practice and design decision-making.
Covers entry and advanced approaches to technology-enabled design.
Students select a Studio 1 course offered by other MDes programs, such as Product Service System Design, Environmental Design, or Media and Communication Design.
Prepares students for ethical academic research and writing.
Supports development of the student’s thesis direction and proposal.
A culminating independent thesis project connected to the student’s professional and research interests.
A co-op or field-practice experience intended to enrich professional and cultural learning.
A cultural studies course offered through Tongji University’s International School.
A Chinese language course offered through Tongji University’s International School.

core_courses.course_title: Tongji University Advanced Lectures for Graduate Students
core_courses.course_summary: A graduate lecture series required as part of the degree structure.

electives.summary: The program lists a broad pool of electives that may vary by semester, including product service system design, environmental design, media and communication design, service design methods, open design, design research, co-creation, user research, experience design, intelligent design, business modeling, information design, AI product design, placemaking, algorithmic design, branding systems, and sustainable design. Five elective credits beyond the specialized studio elective are required.

llm_rationales.note: The program page says TED was developed to address rural and urban changes in Hong Kong and mainland China, focuses on the Greater Bay Area, and frames the specialism as a holistic and systemic response to complex global and local challenges aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals.
Supports social_impact because the page explicitly references rural and urban changes, environmental and ecological design, and responses to complex global and local challenges with SDG alignment.

Supports research because the page describes the program as cultivating intellectual and practical mastery in analysing and designing environments and presents a critical approach to environmental and ecological design.
Supports architecture_adjacent because the program offers training in rural and urban space design and addresses the design of diverse rural and urban environments.

Supports interdisciplinary because the page frames the program around sociocultural dynamics, environmental and ecological design, and systemic responses across local and global contexts, indicating cross-disciplinary positioning. No stronger evidence was found for technology, entrepreneurship, or fine arts adjacency, so those tags were omitted.
The programme page states the normal duration is 1 year (full-time) and 3 years (part-time). Using the standard full-time duration, 1 year corresponds to roughly 2 standard semesters.

The news item confirms the full-time MDes programmes, including Transitional Environments Design, are 1-year programmes and that part-time study extends completion to 3 years, supporting the 1-year baseline used for semester conversion.

positioning.positioning_summary: PolyU’s Transitional Environments Design is positioned as a design-specialist MDes focused on rural and urban change in Hong Kong and mainland China, especially the Greater Bay Area, with a critical environmental and ecological design lens. The program emphasizes systemic responses to complex local and global challenges, including sustainability and regeneration.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Transitional Environments Design MDes is a 37-credit mixed-mode program with a common research/writing spine, a substantial elective component split into two pools, and a specialism sequence that culminates in a capstone research project. The curriculum is described as moving through an intensive course stage, studios and workshops, and a capstone stage, with electives used to customize study and support individual interests. The program emphasizes workshop-based making, collaborative studio work, lab studies, and real-world or industry-sponsored project work.

positioning.positioning_summary: OCAD University’s Strategic Foresight and Innovation (MDes) is positioned as a transdisciplinary, professionally oriented design program centered on foresight, systems thinking, and innovation for business, policy, and social change. The program emphasizes research methods, collaborative work with diverse peers and external professionals, and outcomes such as strategic roadmaps, business models, policy frameworks, and real-world major projects.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program is a 45-credit MDes with a prescribed seven-semester sequence of core courses, a summer flexible slot for independent study, internship, or elective work, and a culminating major project. Electives are available through approved graduate courses, selected undergraduate courses with permission, or external graduate study, but the core curriculum itself is tightly structured.

identity.program_name: Master of Design in Integrated Design

positioning.positioning_summary: The MDes in Integrated Design is positioned as a professional, industry-connected design program that prepares students for strategic and leadership roles through interdisciplinary, studio-based learning. Its emphasis on integrating service, experience, interaction, and transformative design, plus real-world collaboration with industry partners and business-facing problem solving, suggests a strong professional and social-impact orientation rather than a purely academic or technical one.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MDes in Integrated Design is a coursework-based, studio-and-seminar programme centered on integrated design practice, research methods, design strategy, leadership, and collaboration. Evidence shows a structured sequence of named core courses and studios across semesters, plus a broad set of unrestricted electives and internship/research options, but the full degree requirement grid and total programme credits are not fully stated in the fetched material.

core_courses.course_summary: An 8-unit hands-on studio in which students work in small interdisciplinary teams with an industry partner to solve real-world, in-context problems under faculty and industry guidance.
learning_outcomes: Work in small interdisciplinary teams on real-world design problems.
Collaborate with industry partners and faculty mentors.
Apply integrated design approaches across discovery, definition, evaluation, creation, and implementation.
Develop solutions informed by multiple domains and stakeholder perspectives.
Practice professional studio-based problem solving in an applied setting.

core_courses.course_summary: An 8-unit hands-on studio in which students work with an industry partner in small interdisciplinary teams to solve real-world, in-context problems. The course is guided by faculty and industry partner teams and includes project briefs from a range of domains.
learning_outcomes: Work in small interdisciplinary teams on real-world design problems.
Develop solutions with an industry partner in an applied studio setting.
Apply faculty- and industry-guided design practice to project briefs.
Respond to in-context challenges across multiple domains.

core_courses.course_summary: Explores emerging topics in design within a seminar-based core, building on the programme’s focus on strategy, leadership, research methods, and collaboration.
learning_outcomes: Understand emerging issues and directions in design.
Connect design practice with strategy and leadership.
Apply research-informed thinking to design challenges.
Collaborate effectively in seminar and studio settings.

core_courses.course_summary: Addresses the changing role and complexity of design through topics such as systems thinking, value-sensitive design, futures thinking, and design justice.
Focuses on design strategy and leadership for tactical thinking, competitive differentiation, and operating design projects in complex real-world business contexts.
Covers co-creative practices, users and stakeholders, and tools and techniques for successful co-design projects, including service systems and socio-organisational transformation.

electives.summary: The programme offers a broad elective structure rather than a single fixed elective list. Students may choose from Division of Industrial Design electives such as sustainable innovation, healthy living, research immersion, interaction design, creative computing, spatial computing, and independent study, and they may also request non-programme requirement courses from the wider NUS catalogue through the course request process. The evidence indicates electives are meant to broaden or deepen the design curriculum and can support different interests, but no formal concentration or track is stated.

llm_rationales.note: Programme page states 'learn how to use design to improve lives,' 'creating products with purpose,' 'research-based tools and methods,' 'cross-domain knowledge,' alignment with the UN Sustainable Development Goals, and graduates becoming leaders, consultants, studio founders, and start-up founders.
Supported social_impact because the page explicitly frames the programme around improving lives, tackling climate/healthcare/work challenges, and aligning with the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
Supported research because the page highlights 'research-based tools and methods,' 'independent research project,' and 'measure impact' with start-ups, governments, and global agencies.

Supported interdisciplinary because the page says solutions require knowledge from multiple domains and the programme applies experience across disciplinary boundaries; AcrossRCA also requires working across disciplines.

Supported professional because the page explicitly mentions graduates leading in design consultancies, large companies, their own studios, and start-ups, and the curriculum includes commercially sponsored projects and professional skills.
The programme page states '1 year / 45 week programme' and 'This MA is delivered over 45 weeks,' which corresponds to roughly two standard semesters.

Used the programme fee table for September 2026 entry. The page states Overseas and EU tuition as £38,650 for the 1-year MA. This is tuition only; mandatory programme/academic fees are not separately itemized on the page, so the tuition figure is the defensible tuition+fees subtotal available from the source.

Left null because the source provides GBP tuition and mentions additional costs, but does not provide a USD conversion or a separate mandatory programme/academic-fees subtotal suitable for cross-program comparison in USD.

The evidence describes named platforms and a Design Futures pathway, plus thematic emphases that students work through, which supports the presence of distinct emphases/routes within the programme.

Named required curriculum elements were explicitly listed in the source, including Locating Practice, Experimental Design, AcrossRCA, Advanced Practice, Core Skills, and the Independent Research Project, so one row was emitted per named core element.

positioning.positioning_summary: Design Products MA is positioned as a socially engaged, research-led design studio focused on creating products with purpose. The programme emphasizes cross-disciplinary problem solving, user-centred and evidence-based methods, and work that addresses real-world challenges such as climate, healthcare, and systems change, while also supporting professional pathways into consultancies, studios, start-ups, and leadership roles.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Design Products MA is a 1 year / 45 week, 180-credit full-time programme in the School of Design. The curriculum is organized across three terms: Terms 1 and 2 combine short projects, cross-College collaboration, and School-wide units, while Term 3 is an independent research project. The programme emphasizes experimentation, making, critical inquiry, and a final self-directed project, but the fetched material does not provide a complete official module list or a full term-by-term sequence for every component.

core_courses.course_summary: Term 1 unit (15 credits) focused on locating your practice within the design products landscape, strengthening research skills, and connecting research with making.
learning_outcomes: Locate your practice within the design products landscape.
Strengthen research skills for design inquiry.
Connect research and making in practice.
Identify technical and intellectual skills to develop.
Expand experimentation through design methods and approaches.

core_courses.course_summary: Term 2, 15-credit unit focused on advancing academic and professional skills for personal projects and future practice. Themes include Making Meaning, Making Together, and Making Public, with emphasis on inclusive, critical, sustainable, ethical, communicative, entrepreneurial, and leadership skills.
learning_outcomes: Advance academic and professional skills for personal projects.
Develop critical, reflective, and research-informed practice.
Work inclusively and ethically with peers and collaborators.
Position practice within wider cultural and professional contexts.
Strengthen communication, entrepreneurship, and leadership skills.

core_courses.course_summary: A 15-credit unit that advances academic and professional skills for personal projects, practice development, and the transition to independent work in the final term. It emphasizes inclusive, critical, sustainable, and ethical approaches, plus communication, networking, entrepreneurship, and leadership.
learning_outcomes: Develop academic and professional skills for postgraduate practice.
Apply critical, inclusive, sustainable, and ethical approaches.
Strengthen research, reflection, and contextual thinking.
Build collaboration and peer-learning capabilities.
Communicate and position practice within wider ecologies.

core_courses.course_summary: A Term 2 unit that deepens creative methods and application through programme ethos themes and collaboration on a School-wide Grand Challenge.
A 15-credit unit supporting academic and professional skills, including critical thinking, collaboration, sustainability, communication, and leadership.
The Term 3 culminating project in which students independently develop, manage, and deliver a high-resolution Design Products project based on earlier work.

electives.summary: The programme includes a School-wide elective in Term 2, but the fetched material does not provide a full elective policy or a complete list of options. The page indicates that electives are part of the broader School of Design offer and can support students in extending their practice, with the overall curriculum also encouraging cross-disciplinary work and specialism through the final project.

Supported research because the page says students learn from 'leading professionals with key insights at the forefront of service design research' and the curriculum includes design research, user testing, and an Independent Research Project.

Supported professional because the page stresses real-world projects, industry partners, leadership roles, and preparation for future employers, plus graduates in businesses, consultancies, charities, NGOs and government.
Supported interdisciplinary because the page highlights teams from different disciplines, collaborations with organisations, and the AcrossRCA unit supporting work across disciplines.

positioning.positioning_summary: The RCA Service Design MA is positioned as a pioneering, research-informed, project-based design programme focused on service design as a tool for social and organisational change. It emphasizes real-world challenges, collaboration with commercial/public/social organisations, and preparation for leadership roles across diverse sectors, with a strong human-centred and systems-oriented orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Service Design MA is a 180-credit, one-year full-time programme structured across three terms with a clear sequence of core units and required project work. Students begin with foundation and practice units in Term 1, move into contextual and management-oriented work in Term 2, and complete a self-directed Independent Research Project in Term 3. The curriculum also includes cross-college/interdisciplinary electives that students choose alongside the core pathway.

core_courses.course_summary: Introduces the theories and concepts underpinning service and service design practice through a small-scale, analytically focused team project.
learning_outcomes: Understand theories and concepts underpinning service design
Apply service design tools and methods in team projects
Develop practice through live, project-based learning
Use research, prototyping, and user testing to shape services
Work critically with complex social and organizational challenges

core_courses.course_summary: 15-credit core unit. Applies service design tools and methods to a live project in dialogue with industry partners, addressing a selected challenge.
learning_outcomes: Apply service design tools and methods to a live project

core_courses.course_summary: Term 2 core unit examining the relationship between service design practice and context, including economic, technological, social, cultural, and environmental systems, and how these shape implementation of service ideas.
learning_outcomes: Develop a project proposal for the Independent Research Project.
Analyze how service design interacts with wider systems and contexts.

core_courses.course_summary: Develops the proposal for the Independent Research Project by examining how service design practice relates to context and implementation.
A School-wide unit built around a wicked design problem that requires cross-disciplinary problem solving and may involve external partners.
Supports academic and professional skills for personal projects, with emphasis on making meaning, making together, and making public.
A self-directed capstone project that is the core public output of the programme and culminates in a public event.

electives.summary: Electives are structured but flexible: students choose cross-college and school-wide options alongside the core curriculum, with the programme explicitly noting that electives can come from across RCA and that availability may depend on demand. The elective component supports students in tailoring their pathway, but the page does not present a fixed specialization track; instead, electives broaden practice and can be used to shape individual interests.

llm_rationales.note: Programme page describes IED as a 'unique programme where traditional and emerging media combine and boundaries are broken,' with 'experimental teaching,' 'speculative design practices,' 'post-digital and cultural systems theory,' and outcomes spanning startups, games, design studios, commercial applications, cultural and tech sectors.

Supports interdisciplinary, research, critical/speculative design, digital, social impact, and professional positioning through phrases such as 'integrate methods from fields beyond art and design,' 'transdisciplinary knowledge base,' 'experimental post-digital and cultural systems theory,' 'speculative design practices,' 'engage deeply with society and the environment,' and graduate pathways into startups and tech/cultural sectors.

Head of programme states IED is a 'methodology' pioneered at the RCA, 'about designing new experiences of information,' and that the programme works across disciplines and systems, reinforcing interdisciplinary, research-led, critical/speculative, and digital/technology-adjacent positioning.

positioning.positioning_summary: Information Experience Design MA is positioned as a boundary-breaking, interdisciplinary, research-led programme that combines traditional and emerging media to design experiences of information. It emphasizes speculative and experimental practice, systems thinking, and socially engaged work, while also pointing to professional pathways in startups, cultural and tech sectors, and independent creative practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Information Experience Design MA is a 180-credit, one-year full-time programme organized across three terms. The curriculum is built around six named required units: Form & Method, AcrossRCA, Situated Practice, Making Worlds with Others, Core Skills, and an Independent Research Project. The programme emphasizes experimental, transdisciplinary practice, collaborative work, and a final self-directed research project; the page also indicates named pathway areas, but it does not clearly present them as formal specializations.

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational Term 1 unit in experience design and intervention. Students undertake bold rapid-making experiments, build understanding of conceptual and technical tools, and propose their aims, concept summary, and technical summary for the degree project.
learning_outcomes: Develop a key proposition for the degree
Use rapid making to test ideas
Build understanding of conceptual and technical tools
Draft concept and technical summaries
Reflect on outcomes of experiments

core_courses.course_summary: 15-credit cross-disciplinary unit in which students work collaboratively across disciplines to respond to urgent contemporary themes through creative and critical practice.
learning_outcomes: Work collaboratively across disciplines
Use creative and critical thinking to address urgent themes
Reflect on collaboration and your role within a team
Develop a creative response through experimentation and dialogue
Position your practice in a wider college context

core_courses.course_summary: 30-credit Term 2 unit focused on refining a signature practice through more focused making, audience awareness, analysis, and collaboration.
learning_outcomes: Refine a signature practice
Develop more focused making
Strengthen audience awareness
Apply critical analysis to processes
Refine technical and conceptual approaches

core_courses.course_summary: School-wide collaborative unit in which students develop a project with and across peers, emphasizing mutual exchange, public engagement, and socially engaged practice.
learning_outcomes: Develop a collaborative project
Engage in mutual exchange of ideas
Situate practice in public and social contexts

core_courses.course_summary: Skills unit supporting academic and professional development for personal projects and progression into independent work.
learning_outcomes: Build academic and professional skills
Support personal project development
Prepare for independent work in Term 3

core_courses.course_summary: Final self-directed research project that integrates practice and critical discourse, culminating in a substantial research piece and presentation.
learning_outcomes: Complete an independent research project
Integrate practice and theory
Present and reflect on the significance of the work

electives.summary: The programme includes a School-wide elective in Term 2, and the page also describes the degree as using College-wide electives and other cross-school options to support experimentation and collaboration. The evidence does not provide a full elective rule set or a named elective list for this MA, but it does show that electives are part of the curriculum and can broaden study across the RCA.

llm_rationales.note: Programme page says students develop a 'transdisciplinary approach' and strengthen research methodology; core units include research methodologies, complex systems, and envisioning futures, with electives in design resilience, ethics, and innovation, plus professional and entrepreneurial skills.

llm_rationales.note: The programme is described as a 180-credit course, but the source does not specify ECTS or another formal unit system label; RCA pages use credits in a mixed/nonstandard way for this extraction.
The page gives a term-by-term structure: Term 1 core units, Term 2 core units plus proposal, and Term 3 the Independent Research Project, so the curriculum is highly sequenced.

The programme is described as flexible and choice-led, with electives across subjects such as design resilience, design ethics, and design innovation, plus electives from across the College, indicating student choice among distinct elective directions.

Named required curriculum components in the source are Research Methodologies (15 credits), Complex Systems (15 credits), Envisioning Futures, Individual Research Project (IRP) Proposal (15 credits), and Independent Research Project (60 credits).
The programme is 180 credits. Named core units total 120 credits (15+15+15+15+60), leaving 60 credits for electives. Assuming typical 15-credit electives, that implies about 4 elective courses.

The programme page states the MDes Design Futures is a 180-credit programme; RCA pages use credits rather than ECTS or semester credit hours, so the unit system is recorded as credits/other-or-mixed for this extraction.

The page gives a term-by-term structure: Term 1 Research Methodologies and Complex Systems, Term 2 Envisioning Futures and IRP Proposal, and Term 3 Independent Research Project, with electives also placed in Terms 1 and 2. That is a highly sequenced core path.

Named required curriculum components in the fetched material are Research Methodologies, Complex Systems, Envisioning Futures, Individual Research Project (IRP) Proposal, and Independent Research Project. Sequence positions are encoded as integers because the schema requires numeric values.

The programme totals 180 credits. Core credits explicitly stated are 15 + 15 + 15 + 60 = 105, leaving 75 credits for electives and other non-core study. Using a typical 15-credit elective unit size on this RCA page yields an estimate of 5 elective units, but because Envisioning Futures is named without a credit value and the page describes electives broadly, I used a conservative estimate of 4 elective course slots rather than overstate precision.

positioning.positioning_summary: Design Futures is positioned as a research-led, interdisciplinary MDes that uses design futures methods to address complex social, environmental, and strategic uncertainty. The programme emphasizes transdisciplinary practice, research methodology, complex systems, futures thinking, and entrepreneurial/professional skills, with a strong sustainability and ethics orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MDes Design Futures is a 180-credit programme in the School of Design with a mostly fixed three-term structure: Term 1 core units in research and systems, Term 2 work on futures and proposal development, and Term 3 the independent research project. Alongside the core sequence, students choose School-wide and College-wide electives in Terms 1 and 2, making the programme structured but choice-led.

core_courses.course_summary: Term 1 core unit (15 credits) introducing research and futures methodologies to support the imagination and realisation of alternative futures through diverse knowledge, stakeholders, and perspectives.
learning_outcomes: Use research and futures methodologies to imagine alternative futures.
Engage diverse forms of knowledge, stakeholders, and perspectives.
Strengthen research methodology for transdisciplinary design practice.
Develop ideas that address complex social and environmental challenges.
Refine thinking through structured research and critique.

core_courses.course_summary: Term 1 core unit introducing research and futures methodologies to support imagining and realising alternative futures through diverse knowledge, stakeholders, and perspectives. 15 credits.
learning_outcomes: Use research and futures methodologies to support alternative futures work.
Engage diverse forms of knowledge, stakeholders, and perspectives.
Strengthen research methodology for transdisciplinary design practice.
Develop thinking for complex social and environmental challenges.
Contribute to debate, critique, and peer-led learning.

core_courses.course_summary: Core unit in term two combining futures methods with advanced digital and analogue envisioning methods to bring near and deep futures to life from a multi-species perspective.
learning_outcomes: Apply futures methods to imagine alternative futures
Use digital and analogue envisioning techniques
Develop near and deep futures from a multi-species perspective
Translate research into future-oriented design propositions

core_courses.course_summary: Develops a research proposal that demonstrates the need for research on the chosen topic, identifies gaps in knowledge, and explains how the proposal will address them.
The concluding 60-credit independent research project in which students propose and realise a body of work, supported by tutorials and opportunities to share research developments.

electives.summary: Electives are a major part of the programme and are chosen in Terms 1 and 2 from School-wide and College-wide offerings across RCA schools, campuses, and online. Students rank preferences and are allocated based on availability, with no prerequisite for the Term 2 School-wide electives. The elective structure supports a bespoke pathway and lets students shape the programme around interests such as design resilience, ethics, and innovation, but the page does not present a fixed concentration or formal track.
